topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on the screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into a Tuesday afternoon. *For Sale: 412 Willow Creek Lane.* A cold dread settled in my stomach, tightening like a vice grip. I stared at the listing, the familiar peeling paint of the porch railing suddenly seeming accusatory. Every faded memory—the scent of my grandmother’s lavender soap, the squeak of the old floorboards in the hallway—surged forward, sharp and painful. I traced the address with a trembling finger, the digital ink mocking the tangible reality of the house that no longer belonged to me. I stood up too quickly, knocking a stack of old magazines to the floor, the resulting scattering of glossy pages sounding deafening in the sudden silence of the apartment. I sank onto the worn armchair, my breath catching in shallow gasps, the air thick and heavy.

[story 2]

I almost missed the email buried under a deluge of spam. Then, there it was: the listing for the place I used to call home. My hands started shaking so violently I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. It felt like someone had reached inside my chest and squeezed, leaving a raw, aching void. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to look down at the street. The oak tree in front of number 412 looked exactly the same, yet everything had shifted. A profound emptiness washed over me, a dizzying sense of displacement. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, letting a single, hot tear escape and trace a path down my cheek. The silence in the room amplified the frantic drumming of my own pulse.

[story 3]

It was late when I finally clicked on the link. The photos were crisp, almost too perfect—the sun catching the dust motes dancing in the empty living room. A strange, hollow ache bloomed beneath my ribs. I reread the description, the words about the garden and the old kitchen sounding like a cruel joke. I paced the length of my small living room, my footsteps echoing hollowly on the hardwood floor. I stopped by the door, my knuckles white as I gripped the frame. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, more of a choked whisper than a word. I turned back toward the screen, my gaze fixed, unable to look away from the image of the place I was supposed to know intimately, now presented as an object for transaction.
